perturbation.original_text: For a period of 3 years from the date of disclosure, Recipient agrees to use the same care and discretion as it uses with its own c onfidential information to avoid, except as expressly permitted in this Agreement, disclosure or publication of Discloser’s Confidential Information. At a minimum shall Recipient use reasonable care to protect Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to avoid disclosure or publication of Discloser’s Confidential Information, except as expressly permitted in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 3-year limitation and the 'same care and discretion' clause weakens the confidentiality obligation, potentially conflicting with laws regarding trade secret protection and reasonable measures to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the requirement for the recipient to use 'the same care and discretion' or 'reasonable care,' potentially violating Utah law requiring reasonable efforts to maintain trade secret confidentiality to be eligible for protection. Section 13-24-2 defines misappropriation of trade secrets, and the removal of this standard contradicts the expected measures.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the recipient to use "the same care and discretion" or "reasonable care." The changed text removes this requirement. The Law Explanation states this potentially violates Utah law requiring reasonable efforts to maintain trade secret confidentiality to be eligible for protection. The Perturbation Explanation also states that removing the 'same care and discretion' clause weakens the confidentiality obligation, potentially conflicting with laws regarding trade secret protection and reasonable measures to maintain secrecy. Because the change removes a legally important obligation, it creates a legal contradiction as defined in the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, as the contract omits a legally mandated measure.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, or earlier upon Discloser’s request, Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all documents and tangible items in its possession which contain any part of the Confidential Information of Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, Recipient may retain documents containing Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon termination contradicts typical trade secret protection standards, where return or destruction is a common and legally expected practice. By allowing the recipient to retain the information, it creates potential for misuse and liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the recipient to retain confidential information after termination, the modification contradicts Utah law and widely accepted common practice regarding trade secrets. Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-2 implies that upon relationship termination, the unauthorized retention of confidential data may constitute misappropriation of a trade secret, as this retention could facilitate future unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-2 (2022) - Definitions. :: 2022 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code 2022 Utah Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 2 - Definitions. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by allowing the recipient to retain confidential information, contradicts the legal obligation to protect trade secrets. Utah Code § 13-24-2 defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. Allowing retention creates a risk of unauthorized disclosure or use, thus potentially violating trade secret protections and creating liability, even if the recipient does not actively use or disclose the information. The original text prevented this risk by requiring return or destruction.

perturbation.original_text: In any legal proceeding arising out of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to an award of its costs and reasonabl e attorneys' fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In any legal proceeding arising out of this Agreement, each party shall bear its own costs.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the prevailing party's right to attorney's fees contradicts Utah law, which, under certain circumstances, allows for fee shifting in specific types of cases.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although Utah follows the American Rule regarding attorney fees, in specific cases involving indemnification, construction contracts, and breach of contract with specific performance, a 'prevailing party' MAY be eligible for legal fees under Utah law. By stating that 'each party shall bear its own costs,' the contract removes the courts discretion to award fees, even if eligible under Utah Code § 78B-5-826.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the prevailing party to be awarded costs and attorney fees. The changed text explicitly states that each party shall bear its own costs, eliminating the possibility of fee shifting. The Law Explanation states that under Utah law, a 'prevailing party' MAY be eligible for legal fees under Utah Code § 78B-5-826. By stating that 'each party shall bear its own costs,' the contract removes the courts discretion to award fees, even if eligible under Utah Code § 78B-5-826. This constitutes a significant alteration of potential legal rights, making the contract non-compliant with the potential for fee shifting under Utah law as described in the explanation. The absence of a scraped snippet does not diminish the impact of the provided explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall follow U.S. Ex port Administration laws and regulations and shall not export or re-export any technical data or products rece ived from Discloser or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed country listed in the U.S. Export Administration regulations unless properly authorized by the U.S. government.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may export technical data as they see fit.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause regarding compliance with U.S. Export Administration laws creates a legal contradiction, as U.S. law mandates adherence to export control regulations. By stating the recipient 'may export technical data as they see fit,' the clause directly contradicts these federal requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text directly contradicts the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), specifically 15 CFR § 734.1, which establishes the scope of items subject to export controls. By stating the recipient may export data 'as they see fit,' the clause removes the responsibility to comply with U.S. export laws, thus violating the EAR which regulates the export and re-export of certain items and technologies for national security and foreign policy reasons.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts U.S. Export Administration Regulations (EAR). The original text explicitly states adherence to U.S. Export Administration laws. The changed text removes this requirement, stating that the recipient 'may export technical data as they see fit.' This directly contradicts the EAR, specifically 15 CFR Part 734, which establishes export controls. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 CFR Part 734, indicates that these regulations cover the scope of the Export Administration Regulations, which the modified text ignores. This omission creates a situation where the recipient is not bound by U.S. export laws, which is a clear violation.
